channel.title: Progression on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Progression on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Arcanum Experience Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the Arcanum experience point system, the design behind it and how it’s kind of contentious.

It was to the dev team back in the day and players today still talk about it. So let’s dive into it. This actually comes from a question from blah vlah, who asked: “Can you talk about the experience system in Arcanum? I’m not sure there’s another game with similar displacement of XP, being mostly on a per hit basis, with a pretty minor emphasis on the per kill point. This makes most solo builds very strong, but politician characters that collect a lot of followers or mages that specialize in summoning get very little experience. What was your design philosophy here? It’s a good question.

item.title: Skill Trees
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about skill trees.

This is a question from every Nova, not any Nova. Every Nova who asked: “Can you please record a video about RPG skill trees? I’m interested specifically in the skill tree as a model for acquiring skills. Different types of skill trees, different point allocation models, even different shapes of skill trees as a different way to force the player into specific choices” sometimes.

item.title: The Million XP Dungeon
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the million experience Point dungeon, or, based on how I’m dressed, this could also be called The Old Man and the C++- C++.

Anyway, the million XP dungeon is something I heard about over three decades ago. I think I heard about it in 1993. I was either at Gateway or orcon up in Los Angeles. Those were gaming conventions.

item.title: Grind
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about grind specifically.

Raul jandes asked about grind and in fact he even said I thought you had done one. I looked at a lot of your videos. I couldn’t see it I thought I had done one too, and I think what’s happening is I’ve done so many videos now that buried in some video I have that’s on a completely different topic. I may go off on a tangent for a minute and talk about grind, but I haven’t actually done a video on grind, so let’s do it. First I’m going to Define it, because I’ve learned I need to Define my terms clearly. Then I’m going to talk about um, how it’s different for different people and why it even exists and if you can make a game without it.

item.title: Skill-based XP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about skill-based XP.

I did a video a few weeks ago where I talked about what I thought the best way to give XP was, which spoiler warning is: through quests, because that’s the way I thought most encouraged players of any build to advance through your game. This generated a ton of discussion — really good discussion, by the way. It was a perfect example of why I like doing this channel. I was reading all these comments, I responded to some of them and it made me think, so I thought, hey, let’s do a video on skill-based XP. Um, I now I’ve done a video on class based versus skill-based systems that you also may want to watch. It’s these videos will be linked below. Um, you just may want to watch it first to know what I’m talking about, but I’m at a high level.

item.title: How To Give Experience Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about how to give XP experience points.

This is kind of an extension of a video I did earlier — and I’ll link it below — on experience and level progression, but that was about how I recommended you give it out. But Gabriel matusovich — I hope that’s the right pronunciation — asked me how to give out XP such that different player builds would gain XP at the same rate. So no matter how you play the game, you’d roughly be the same level as you progress through it. So what we’re really talking here is the rate of XP gain in an RPG.

item.title: Enemy Progression
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about enemy progression.

Stagi Ayatollah asks: well, says a good topic for the youngans might be beneficial — is your thoughts on how to handle enemy scaling, something that’s fumbled a lot these days, IMO, perhaps in a border topic of how to efficiently handle enemies in general. I replied to this one and said I didn’t really want to talk about enemy scaling, but I would talk about enemy progression because I’ve mentioned in another video that I don’t like level scaling. I don’t like it when the enemies are adjusted automatically to fit your level and I also don’t like that how it’s often done, just as here are a bunch of extra hit points and better armor and better weapons, to the point where you’re wondering why is a bandit who’s literally carrying around 40,000 gold worth of weapons and armor attacking me?

item.title: Level Caps
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about level caps and this is a question from my industry buddy, Matt Pritchard, and he basically wanted me to talk about level caps versus having infinite advancement in an RPG.

Now, if you’ve played any of my games, you’ll know that I always seem to go level cap and mainly it’s because I’ve always pushed as one of the design pillars — even if it isn’t expressly stated in some of my games — that I want the player to have a lot of opportunity to have replayability in my game, to make new characters and experience new content and do different things, basically play differently and see how that works out. The problem I’ve always had with infinite advancement is you will eventually get all your skills to Max. You get all the perks that are in the game. You’ll see all the content, or I suppose I should caveat that you’re going to see most content.

item.title: Experience & Level Progression
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about leveling and progression, and this is actually me answering a question from — and I hope I’m pronouncing this user’s name right — stavrus nicolao.

He basically asks: I would love to hear your thoughts on experiencing it. Experience leveling, progression, design, how XP is gained, how it’s spent, progression restrictions, Etc. So got a lot of thoughts on that.
